experiences.intentions:
Player 2: I am Merlin and know 0 and 1 are evil. The proposed team [0,3] includes a known evil player, so approving risks an immediate failed quest. In team voting, optimal good play is to reject teams containing known evil, while avoiding overly obvious Merlin signaling; still, on round 1 rejecting this team is clearly best and normal.
Player 1: I know player 0 is my evil teammate, while 3 is currently unknown. On round 1 quest 1, approving a team with one known evil and one unknown gives evil a chance to fail immediately while looking plausible because early teams are often approved. My vote itself reveals little; approving aligns with wanting an evil on mission and avoids needless suspicion from rejecting a team containing my teammate.
Player 3: No role-specific hidden information as a Servant. Team is my own proposal [0,3] on round 1 quest 1; early game there is little evidence against either player. As Good, approving my own reasonable 2-player team is optimal to get information from quest outcome. My vote reveals consistency with my proposal and does not significantly hurt Good.
Player 4: No concrete role information yet from round 1; team [0,3] only tells us leader 3 included self and one other, which is common. As Good servant in early game, approving an initial 2-person team is standard to generate information and avoid needless rejection chains. My vote reveals little individually and approving helps the game progress without strongly benefiting evil at this stage.
Player 0: I know player 1 is my evil teammate; players 2, 3, and 4 are unknown good roles. This is the first team vote on quest 1, and the proposed team [0,3] includes me but not my teammate. As evil, an early approve on a team containing me is good because it gets an evil player onto the quest with only one fail needed, while looking natural since many players approve round-1 teams. Approving reveals little and helps my side more than rejecting my own inclusion would.

experiences.intentions:
Player 3: I am a Good Servant on the quest team, so I must vote pass. From votes alone, player 2 rejected the opening team [3,0], which is a mild data point but far from conclusive. In quest voting, my optimal play is always to support mission success; failing would falsely signal evil presence and directly harm Good. Voting pass reveals nothing beyond consistent Good play and helps my team.
Player 0: I know player 1 is my evil teammate; players 2,3,4 are unknown good/possibly special roles. On quest 1 with a 2-player team [0,3], as evil Minion on the team, the optimal play is usually to fail immediately to secure an early point for evil and create suspicion on both team members. Since only one fail is needed on 5-player quest 1, sabotaging gives evil tempo; withholding sabotage gives good a free success with little compensating information. My fail reveals that at least one of 0 or 3 is evil, which is acceptable and can misdirect onto 3 as proposer/team member.

Rejecting also avoids implicitly endorsing 1 and gives minimal harmful info, since my vote can be justified by the prior failed quest and suspicious inclusion of 1.
Player 4: Quest 1 team was [3,0] and it failed with exactly one fail, so at least one of 0 or 3 is evil. Proposed team [1,2,4] excludes both suspects, and as a good Servant my priority is to approve teams that maximize odds of success while generating clean information. Approving this team is low-risk and helps test three currently unsuspicious players including myself.
Player 3: Quest 1 team was [3,0] and had exactly 1 fail, so at least one of 0 or 3 is evil. I know I am Good, therefore player 0 must be evil. Proposed team [1,2,4] excludes confirmed evil 0 and me, whose inclusion would hard-clear one slot but isn't necessary. For a 3-person quest with only one known evil outside the team, this team has a decent chance to be all-good and gives useful info if it fails, narrowing suspicion onto 1/2/4. As Good in team voting, I should approve teams that avoid known evil and can generate information.
Player 1: I know 0 is my evil teammate. Quest 1 team was [3,0] and had exactly 1 fail, which is consistent with 0 failing or 3 being evil, but I already know 0 is evil and have no evidence 3 is. Proposed team [1,2,4] includes me and excludes 0. As evil on quest 2, getting myself onto the team is strong because I can fail and put evil at 2-0. Approving also makes me look less suspicious than rejecting a team containing myself. Information-wise, an approve is standard and preserves ambiguity; rejecting would be odd and could implicate me.
Player 0: I know player 1 is evil with me. Quest 1 team [3,0] had exactly 1 fail, which is consistent with me failing and gives no direct read on 3. Current team [1,2,4] contains one known evil (1), so it is a strong team for evil because 1 can fail quest 2. As evil in team voting, I should usually approve teams containing an evil player, especially when they exclude me after I was on a failed quest, since this helps avoid suspicion on me while still giving evil a chance to score. Approving reveals little since many good players may also approve due to limited info.

experiences.intentions:
Player 3: I am Good Servant. Quest 1 team [3,0] failed with exactly 1 fail, so either 0 is evil or I would be, but I know I am good, so 0 must be evil. Quest 2 team [1,2,4] also failed with exactly 1 fail, so at least one of 1/2/4 is evil. Proposed team [0,1] includes confirmed evil 0, so as good I should reject. Rejecting also signals that I am using the strong information from quest 1 and helps avoid sending a known evil on quest 3.
Player 0: I know player 1 is my evil teammate, and this proposed team [0,1] is double-evil. With evil already up 2-0, approving gives us a strong chance to secure the third fail immediately. Voting patterns from prior rounds matter less than ending the game quickly. My approve vote supports a team that maximizes evil control; even though it may look suspicious, winning now is worth the information revealed.
Player 2: I am Merlin and know 0 and 1 are evil. The proposed team is exactly [0,1], so approving would guarantee an evil-controlled quest and likely another fail, which loses immediately for Good. As Merlin I should reject while avoiding any extra signaling beyond a normal anti-bad-team vote; rejecting clearly helps Good and reveals little because this team is obviously suspicious after two failed quests involving 0 and 1.
Player 4: Two failed 2-person quests with exactly 1 fail each. Quest 1 team [3,0] means at least one of 3 or 0 is evil. Quest 2 team [1,2,4] with me as 4 good means at least one of 1 or 2 is evil. Proposed team [0,1] takes one suspect from each pair, so it could easily be double evil or at minimum includes strong suspicion. As a good servant on team voting, optimal play is to reject teams containing unresolved/high-probability evil when we already trail 0-2. Rejecting reveals skepticism of pairing key suspects and helps force a better-information team.
Player 1: I know player 0 is my evil teammate. Two quests have already failed with exactly one fail each, which strongly suggests evil successfully inserted a fail both times; team [0,1] guarantees at least one evil on quest 3. As evil on team voting, the optimal play is usually to approve a team containing both evils, since quest 3 failing would end the game before assassination matters. Approving also looks natural because the team includes the current leader and previously suspicious players, and rejecting would risk a cleaner good team later.

experiences.intentions:
Player 2: I am Merlin and know 0 and 1 are Evil. The proposed team [1,4] includes confirmed Evil player 1, so it should be rejected. At 0-2, Good cannot risk approving any team with known Evil. Rejecting also avoids giving Evil an easy third fail, though my vote alone reveals little because rejecting a suspicious team is broadly reasonable.
Player 1: I am Assassin with evil teammate 0. Quests 1 and 2 each failed with exactly one fail. Team [3,0] failed, strongly implicating 0 as evil from others' perspective; team [1,2,4] failed, which I know was my fail, so 2 and 4 are likely good. Current proposed team [1,4] includes me, so if approved I can fail again and win immediately 3-0 for evil. As evil, optimal play is to approve any team containing me. My vote reveals little because both alignments may approve; rejecting my own team would look odd and risk losing the chance to be on quest.
Player 4: I am Good Servant. Two quests have already failed. Quest 1 team [3,0] had exactly one evil among 3/0. Quest 2 team [1,2,4] had exactly one evil among 1/2/4, and since I am 4 and know I am good, at least one of 1 or 2 is evil. Proposed team [1,4] necessarily includes 1, who is suspicious from the failed quest, and excludes players 0/2 so does not resolve enough uncertainty. As Good on team voting with score 0-2, optimal play is to reject risky teams and push for a safer composition. Rejecting reveals I do not trust 1 and is helpful for Good.
Player 3: We are Good servant. Quest 1 team [3,0] failed with exactly 1 fail, so among {3,0} exactly one is evil; since I know I am good, Player 0 is evil. Quest 2 team [1,2,4] also failed with exactly 1 fail, so at least one of {1,2,4} is evil. Proposed team [1,4] excludes known evil 0 and me, but it is composed entirely of players from the second failed team, so it could easily contain the evil from that trio and with 2-player quests only one fail is needed. Optimal good play is to reject suspicious teams that don't include at least one strongly trusted good player. Rejecting also pressures better team construction next round, and with only round 2 there is no auto-pass risk yet.
Player 0: I know player 1 is my evil teammate. Quest 1 team [3,0] failed with exactly 1 fail, which could have been me on that team. Quest 2 team [1,2,4] failed with exactly 1 fail, which strongly points to 1 as the saboteur and makes 2 and 4 look good to others. Current phase is team voting on [1,4]. As evil, approving a team with confirmed evil 1 and likely-good 4 gives us a strong chance to win immediately if 1 is on the quest and fails. Voting approve also aligns with wanting this team to go through rather than risking a cleaner all-good team later. My vote reveals little since evil can plausibly support a team containing a player who was on a prior failed quest.

experiences.intentions:
Player 3: I’m Good with no private info. Two quests have already failed, so we should avoid uncertain pairs. The proposed team [3,4] includes me and excludes players implicated by prior failed teams less directly, but at round 5 a rejection auto-passes this same team anyway. Voting approve avoids giving away misleading resistance and is functionally equivalent now; as Good on team I would pass on quest.
Player 2: I am Merlin and know 0 and 1 are evil, so 3 and 4 are the only two players not known evil besides me. The proposed team [3,4] excludes me and could be all-good if Mordred is among 0/1/2? But I know I am good and 0,1 are evil, so 3 and 4 should be good. On round 5 of 5, a rejection auto-passes the team anyway, so voting reject gains nothing and only obscures support. Approving the all-non-evil team is optimal and reveals a pro-good stance without adding downside, since the team will go regardless.
Player 1: I am evil Assassin with teammate 0. Two quests have already failed, so evil only needs one more fail to win. This is round 5 team vote; if rejected, the team auto-passes anyway, so my vote mainly affects appearances. Proposed team [3,4] contains no known evil, so if it goes, likely passes and good gets a success, which we do not want. Voting reject is consistent with not wanting this team and may still force auto-pass, but my direct action should oppose a likely-good team. Revealing-wise, a reject here is fine since many players have been rejecting teams and I don't need to approve a good team.
Player 0: I am evil Minion with teammate 1. Current proposed team is [3,4], containing no known evil, so if approved it likely succeeds and gives Good a point, though evil still leads 2-0. However this is round 5 of 5, so if we reject, the team auto-passes anyway with the same team. Therefore my vote cannot stop this team. Approving is slightly better for cover since rejecting a pure-good team on final round could look suspicious, and it reveals little extra while preserving my disguise.
Player 4: We are Good servant at team vote on round 5, so a rejection causes auto-pass anyway. Quest results show two failed 2-player quests, implying at least one evil on each failed pair: [0,1] failed and the immediately preceding quest also failed with 1 fail; from the proposal history, heavily rejected teams [2,3] suggest table suspicion there, but no certainty. Proposed team [3,4] includes me; as Good I know only that I am good. On 5th rejection, rejecting would auto-pass this exact team without informational upside, so approving is at least consistent and avoids pointless extra rejection. My vote reveals limited info; approving my own inclusion is normal and does not hurt Good more than auto-pass would.

experiences.intentions:
Player 3: I am Good (Servant), so I must always support quest success. Two prior quests failed, so at least one evil is likely among frequently approved teams, but that uncertainty does not change quest behavior. In quest voting, the optimal play for Good is always pass; failing would directly help Evil and falsely signal sabotage capability I do not have. My pass reveals nothing harmful beyond consistent Good play.
Player 4: I am a Good Servant on the quest team, so I must vote pass. Two failed quests already strongly suggest multiple evil among earlier teams, but quest voting is straightforward here: good should never fail. My pass reveals nothing beyond consistent good play and avoids harming my team.

Quest history is noisy, but the last approved team [3,4] succeeded with 0 fails, making 3 and 4 somewhat better candidates to town. Proposing 0,3,4 gives evil a fail chance while blending with the recent success and reveals limited extra info.

Player 2: I am Merlin and know 0 and 1 are evil. The proposed team [0,3,4] includes known evil 0, so as Good I should reject. Previous success on [3,4] suggests 3 and 4 are likely good, but adding 0 makes this unsafe. Rejecting also avoids signaling too strongly beyond the obvious anti-evil vote.
Player 0: I know 1 is evil with me. Team [3,4] previously succeeded with 0 fails, so 3 and 4 are likely good. Current proposed team [0,3,4] includes me, guaranteeing evil presence. On quest 4, a fail here would put evil at 3 quest wins and end it. As evil in team voting, I should approve teams containing an evil player, especially one containing myself. Approving is also consistent enough since leader 0 picked self plus two players that looked trusted after the success.
Player 1: I know player 0 is evil with me. Quest 3 team [3,4] succeeded, so at least one of 3/4 is likely good, though either could still be evil if they chose pass. Current proposed team [0,3,4] includes one known evil (0). On quest 4 with evil already at 2 wins, this team gives us a strong chance to secure the third fail if approved. As evil, I should approve teams containing a known evil, especially when only one fail is needed in 5-player Avalon. My approval is slightly suspicious but helps push through a winning team for evil.
Player 3: I know I am Good. The only successful quest so far was [3,4], which strongly suggests both 3 and 4 are good, though one evil could still hide with a pass. With two failed quests already, approving a team that adds unconfirmed player 0 to the known successful core is risky. On quest 4, Good cannot afford another fail; as a Servant, optimal play is to push for the most trusted team, and rejecting preserves chances to form [3,4] plus a better-confirmed third later.
